title: The message that filed itself under the wrong session
logline: A debounce timer and a rejected-submit restore both wrote drafts using whatever session was focused *when they fired*, not the one that owned the text — so a long, careful message vanished into another chat.

intro.one_liner: A ref updated only by the session-switch effect, not on every render — so async code reads the session that's actually loaded, not whatever's focused right now.
intro.full: `activeQueueSessionKeyRef` gets reassigned on every render, so by the time an async callback (a debounce timer, a `pagehide` handler) reads it, it may already point at whatever session the user switched to — not the one whose text it's supposed to persist. `draftScopeRef` fixes this by only being written inside the swap effect, the one place that actually owns 'which session's draft is loaded right now.' Anything scheduled while a session is loaded captures that ref's value and keeps it, even if the render-time ref moves on underneath it.

intro.one_liner: A token captured at submit time that identifies which session a reply belongs to, checked again before delivery.
intro.full: When a prompt is submitted, the code now grabs `getRouteToken()` and the current stored session id at that moment. Async resume, attachment sync, and timeout recovery use those captured values instead of re-reading 'what session is focused now.' If the token no longer matches by the time a response is ready to land, the delivery is treated as stale and rolled back instead of appended to whatever chat happens to be open.

intro.one_liner: Before a delayed write commits, it checks it's still the pending write on file — not superseded by a clear or a newer one.
intro.full: `isPendingDraftPersistCurrent` compares a `{scope, text}` pair against whatever's currently recorded as pending. A debounce timer captures its pair when scheduled, but doesn't fire until later — by then a session swap may have cleared the pending slot, or a newer keystroke may have replaced it. The guard drops the write in either case instead of letting a stale timer overwrite fresh text. It's defense-in-depth: the scope-capture fix already makes every captured pair correct, this just stops a future regression from silently misfiling one session's text under another's key.

cast.label: @HexLab98
cast.role: author of the submit/resume routing fix

cast.label: @JoaoMarcos44
cast.role: author of the composer draft-scope fix

panels.body: Two Hermes Desktop sessions are open: **Session A**, an existing Flappy Royale chat, and **Session B**, a fresh session for a new project. The user types a long, carefully composed prompt into Session B — then switches to check Session A.

figure.caption: The message that never made it

steps.note: Session A is open — an existing project chat
Session B opens — a fresh session
User types a long message into Session B
User switches focus to Session A before the debounce fires

steps.label: stashAt(scope, text)
steps.note: Debounce timer fires — reads the render-time ref, which now says Session A
Session B's message is filed under Session A's key and never seen again

panels.body: The linked bug, #54527, reports the loss as **permanent**: the text typed into Session B never appears in any session transcript or the message database. Two independent contributors filed fixes for two different manifestations of the same root shape.

The draft debounce, page-hide flush, and rejected-submit restore all needed to answer one question: **which session's text is this?** Before this PR, they answered it by reading a ref that updates on every render — `activeQueueSessionKeyRef` — at the moment the async work finally runs, not at the moment it was scheduled.

**@JoaoMarcos44's angle:** the bug is in the composer. The debounce timer and `pagehide` flush capture `activeQueueSessionKeyRef.current` when they fire, but the user may have switched sessions in the meantime — the ref has moved on. Fix: pin persistence to the session that's actually loaded in the editor, via a **swap-owned ref**.

**@HexLab98's angle:** the bug is in submit/resume routing. Async resume, attachment sync, and timeout recovery for a submitted prompt can complete well after the user has switched sessions — and reconnect to whatever session is now focused, delivering the reply into the wrong chat. Fix: pin the starting stored session and a **route token** captured at submit time, and abort delivery on drift.

A maintainer comment on the PR cluster confirms both are real, independent races on the same underlying shape: async work started under one session, completing after focus moved to another. #62134 (HexLab98) and #62144 (JoaoMarcos44) each fixed one half. Neither fixed the other.
Before the reveal — given both hypotheses above, what should the merged fix actually do?

prediction.question: Given draft-scope pinning fixes the composer half and route-token pinning fixes the submit half, what should PR #62413 do?

options.text: Merge whichever branch is more complete and close the other
Take both mechanisms — draft-scope pinning AND route-token pinning — without adopting either branch wholesale
Write one new shared abstraction that replaces both refs with a single session-locking hook

feedback.pick-one: Tempting, but the two fixes cover different code paths (composer drafts vs. submit/resume routing) — picking one leaves the other race live.
feedback.combine-both: Correct. The PR body states it explicitly: combine the two independent fixes 'without taking either contributor's broader branch wholesale.' Each mechanism is minimal and scoped to its own path.
feedback.rewrite-shared: Half credit for the instinct — a shared abstraction is plausible engineering, but nothing in the PR data suggests one was built. The actual fix keeps the two mechanisms separate.

panels.body: PR #62413 combines both: draft debounce, page-hide flush, and rejected-submit restore now use `draftScopeRef` (the composer scope that owns the loaded text); async resume, attachment sync, and timeout recovery retain the starting stored session and a `getRouteToken()` value captured at submit time. If the route drifts, the optimistic message is aborted and rolled back instead of delivered into another chat.

steps.note: Session B loads; swap effect sets draftScopeRef = 'session-b'
User types; debounce captures {scope: draftScopeRef.current, text}
User switches focus to Session A

steps.label: stashAt('session-b', text)
steps.note: Timer fires — still reads the captured scope, not the new focus
Session A's own swap effect clears any stale pending write and sets its own scope

panels.body: The integrity guard is the defense-in-depth layer: even with correct scope capture, it double-checks nothing cleared or replaced the pending write before committing.

code.caption: The debounce timer checks this before committing — if the pending slot was cleared by a session swap or replaced by a newer keystroke, the write is dropped instead of filing text under the wrong session.

panels.body: Focused desktop UI tests: 60 passed. Desktop TypeScript: clean. The submit-side fix also threads `getRouteToken` through `desktop-controller.tsx` into the prompt-actions hook, and the test harness gained explicit `activeSessionIdRef`/`selectedStoredSessionIdRef`/`getRouteToken` params to exercise the drift case.

before.title: Before
items: Draft persistence reads activeQueueSessionKeyRef.current at fire time
Submit restore re-stashes into whatever session is currently focused
Async resume/attachment sync reconnect to the current session, no drift check

after.title: After
items: Draft persistence uses draftScopeRef, set only by the swap effect
Rejected-submit restore uses the scope captured at dispatch
Async resume/attachment sync/timeout recovery pin session + route token; drift aborts and rolls back

panels.body: This closes #54527 and supersedes both partial fixes, #62134 and #62144, per the maintainer's own flag on the PR cluster.

The integrity guard exists so a *future* regression — a call site that skips the scope-capture bookkeeping — fails safe by dropping the write rather than silently misrouting it again. Whether that guard ever fires in practice is an open question for whoever touches this code next.

decisions.chose: Combine both independent fixes (draft-scope pinning + route-token pinning) into one PR without adopting either branch wholesale
decisions.over: Merging one of #62134 or #62144 and closing the other
decisions.because: The two fixes address different code paths (composer draft persistence vs. submit/resume routing) — either alone leaves half the race live.

decisions.chose: Add isPendingDraftPersistCurrent as a defense-in-depth integrity guard on top of the scope-capture fix
decisions.over: Relying solely on draftScopeRef being correct
decisions.because: Protects against a future call site reintroducing a stale/live-ref read without full test coverage catching it.

boundaries: The integrity guard only checks scope+text equality on the pending slot — it doesn't cover every possible future call site that might bypass draftScopeRef entirely.
Route-token pinning aborts and rolls back on drift; it does not queue or redeliver the message to the correct session automatically.

quiz.question: A new async operation in the composer needs to persist text after a delay. Where should it read 'which session owns this text' from?

options.text: activeQueueSessionKeyRef.current, read when the operation finally runs
A ref set only by the session-swap effect, captured when the operation is scheduled
A prop passed down from the parent, re-read at render time

feedback.render-ref: This is exactly the bug this PR fixes — a ref updated on every render can point at a different session by the time delayed async work reads it.
feedback.swap-ref: Correct — mirrors draftScopeRef: only the swap effect owns 'which session is actually loaded,' so async work should capture that value, not poll a render-time ref later.
feedback.prop: Props re-render just like the ref does — same staleness problem, just relocated.

quiz.question: A submitted prompt's response arrives after the user switched sessions. What should happen?

options.text: Deliver it into whichever session is currently focused
Check the captured route token; if it no longer matches, abort and roll back the optimistic message
Silently queue it for the next time the original session is focused

feedback.deliver-current: This is the exact misrouting bug from #54527 — a reply lands in a chat that never asked for it.
feedback.abort-rollback: Correct — this PR's route-token pinning aborts and rolls back on drift rather than delivering into the wrong chat.
feedback.queue-both: Plausible-sounding but not what was built here; the PR data shows abort+rollback, not silent queuing — inventing a queue mechanism isn't supported by the source.

quiz.question: Why add isPendingDraftPersistCurrent when draftScopeRef already captures the correct scope at schedule time?

options.text: It's redundant and could be removed without risk
It's a safety net: if a future call site skips scope-capture correctness, the guard drops the write instead of silently misfiling it
It exists to batch multiple pending writes for performance

feedback.redundant: The PR's own comment explains the intent: it's deliberate defense-in-depth against a future regression, not dead weight.
feedback.defense-in-depth: Correct — the code comment states this directly: it's there so a future stale-ref reintroduction fails safe (dropped write) instead of silently misrouting text.
feedback.performance: No batching logic here — it's a pure equality check gating a single commit, not a performance optimization.

quiz.question: Two contributors each fix half of the same bug shape in separate PRs. What's the right move for a maintainer consolidating them?

options.text: Merge whichever PR was opened first
Combine the distinct mechanisms from each into one PR, crediting both authors, without pulling in either branch's full unrelated scope
Ask one contributor to rewrite their PR to include the other's changes

feedback.merge-first: Ignores that the two fixes are complementary, not competing — merging only one leaves a real race unfixed.
feedback.cherry-pick-mechanisms: Correct — this is what #62413 did, per its own body: combine both mechanisms with explicit per-commit attribution, without taking either branch wholesale.
feedback.ask-rewrite: Slower and unnecessary when the two fixes are independent and can be combined directly by whoever's consolidating.

map_updates.note: Draft persistence (debounce, pagehide flush, rejected-submit restore) now pins to draftScopeRef, a ref owned exclusively by the session-swap effect, plus isPendingDraftPersistCurrent as an integrity guard before any delayed write commits.

Prompt submission now captures the starting stored session and a route token (getRouteToken) at dispatch; async resume, attachment sync, and timeout recovery check for drift and abort+rollback rather than deliver into a different session.

teaser: The integrity guard is a tripwire for a regression that hasn't happened yet — the real test of this fix is whether the next call site added to the composer remembers to route through draftScopeRef at all.
